title: Decompression bomb: image::load_from_memory called before dimension check, 10MB compressed can produce multi-GB decoded canvas
description:
src/media.rs process_image() calls image::load_from_memory(bytes) and *then* checks dimensions against MAX_DIMENSION=4096. The decode step allocates pixel memory proportional to the uncompressed image size before the bounds check runs.

A valid PNG within the 10MB download limit can encode a 65535x65535 grayscale image (max PNG dimensions): decoded size = 65535 * 65535 * 4 bytes (RGBA) = ~17GB of RAM. The image crate will attempt to allocate this before returning the DynamicImage that would then be checked.

This is exploitable via:
- Webhook: POST with media URL pointing to a crafted PNG on an attacker server
- Feed: enclosure media URL in a feed entry
- Directory watcher: process_local() has the same path via store_processed_image -> process_image

image 0.25 does perform some sanity checks on PNG chunk headers but does not cap total decoded pixel count by default.

Mitigation: use image::io::Reader with explicit limits before decoding, or use image::ImageDecoder::set_limits() with Limits::default() which caps total pixel count.
close_reason: All pentest fixes: require Signature header, verify Digest, enforce actor-keyId match, Date freshness, body size limit 256KB, webhook key via header, decompression bomb limits, symlink rejection, redirect following disabled, delivery SSRF recheck, backslash escape handling
